domain_coverage.quality: 239 rules — Manufacturing, analytical methods, dissolution, data integrity, formulation, terminology management
domain_coverage.safety: 221 rules — Genotoxicity, mutagenicity, drug interactions, adverse events, NTI, post-market surveillance, MedDRA coding
domain_coverage.efficacy: 157 rules — Bioequivalence, bioavailability, PK/PD, clinical endpoints, bioanalytical validation, clinical protocols
domain_coverage.regulatory: 183 rules — eCTD submission, GCP/GLP compliance, terminology governance, lifecycle management, RWD evidence standards

rules.requirement: Poor quality in the initial data collection and subsequent coding and analysis steps can compromise the ability to evaluate safety issues with regulated products. The companion document would include a section on data quality in regulatory submissions - both in clinical trials and postmarketing.
cross_references: ICH M1 MedDRA Term Selection: Points to Consider
ICH M1 MedDRA Data Retrieval and Presentation: Points to Consider
rules.rationale: Bridges quality of data coding practices with downstream safety signal detection in regulatory submissions.

rules.requirement: There is considerable demand from MedDRA users for detailed examples and Q&As on special topics of regulatory importance such as medication errors and product quality issues.
cross_references: ICH M1 MedDRA Term Selection: Points to Consider
rules.rationale: Medication errors and product quality issues simultaneously implicate product quality standards, patient safety outcomes, and regulatory reporting obligations.

rules.requirement: In 2013, the remit of the PtC WG was extended to allow the WG to provide guidance on MedDRA initiatives on an as-needed basis, including input on the EU Good Practice Guides on Medication Errors.
cross_references: EU Good Practice Guides on Medication Errors (2015)
rules.rationale: Medication error guidance sits at the intersection of patient safety, quality systems, and regulatory requirements.

rules.requirement: The proposed companion document will include a section on data quality in regulatory submissions - both in clinical trials and postmarketing - where poor quality in initial data collection and subsequent coding and analysis steps can compromise the ability to evaluate safety issues.
cross_references: ICH M1 MedDRA Term Selection: Points to Consider
rules.rationale: Data quality at collection and coding stage directly affects postmarketing safety signal detection and clinical trial outcomes assessment within regulatory submissions.

rules.requirement: In situations where the PtC WG does not possess the necessary expertise, consultation with outside experts including from other ICH Working Groups is needed.
rules.rationale: Requirement to draw experts from Q, S, and E series Working Groups acknowledges some MedDRA guidance questions cannot be answered from a single disciplinary standpoint.

rules.requirement: The PtC documents provide guidance on general term selection and data retrieval principles. However, certain topics require more detailed guidance including data quality in regulatory submissions where poor quality in initial data collection compromises safety evaluation.
cross_references: ICH M1 MedDRA Term Selection: Points to Consider
rules.rationale: Adequacy of MedDRA coding quality is inseparable from regulatory submission integrity and safety evaluation capability.

rules.requirement: The MedDRA Management Committee provides oversight of the MedDRA PtC WG for operational matters. However, the ICH Management Committee oversees activities that relate to Guideline development.
rules.rationale: Dual-committee oversight reflects the multidisciplinary nature: operational coding quality under MedDRA MC, guideline development under ICH MC.

rules.requirement: PtC documents with major changes will be signed off by ICH Members and endorsed by the MedDRA MC. Minor changes will be agreed between Rapporteur/Co-Rapporteur and Regulatory Chair.
rules.rationale: Tiered sign-off requirement is calibrated to magnitude of changes to coding guidance affecting safety reporting and quality of regulatory submissions.

rules.requirement: Release of Companion document v2.0 with a new section on product quality issues focusing on distributed (marketed/manufactured) products.
rules.rationale: Adding product quality issues for marketed products into MedDRA coding guidance links quality defects with safety coding and regulatory reporting.

rules.requirement: Release of v3.0 of the Companion document with new section on Manufacturing and Quality System Issues and revised sections on Data quality, Medication errors, and Product quality issues.
rules.rationale: Manufacturing and Quality System Issues section places quality-system concepts inside MedDRA adverse event coding guidance document.

rules.requirement: Release of Companion document v4.0 with additional guidance on terms relating to Manufacturing and Quality System Issues relevant to large molecule, biologics, vaccines, and drug delivery devices.
rules.rationale: Guidance for biologics/vaccines/devices requires simultaneous quality, safety, and regulatory competency across multiple product-class frameworks.

rules.requirement: MedDRA experts in PtC WG will review user comments on current PtC documents and MedDRA updates and check for consistency with the proposed changes to the Companion Document.
cross_references: ICH M1 MedDRA Term Selection: Points to Consider
rules.rationale: Cross-checking consistency across PtC and Companion documents spanning safety, quality, and regulatory topics is a multidisciplinary consistency obligation.

rules.requirement: There is a need to communicate information on medicinal products rapidly from Sponsors to Regulatory Authorities and to disseminate information on safety between Authorities using electronic communication with a common set of standards ensuring integrity of information and data exchange.
rules.rationale: M2 electronic standards are motivated by transmitting safety information reliably to regulatory authorities - serving safety communication, regulatory compliance, and data quality simultaneously.

rules.requirement: Traditionally, the majority of information on the quality, safety and efficacy of medicinal products is supplied to Authorities in paper form. Electronic standards will enable safety updates or submissions to be provided instantaneously to many Authorities.
rules.rationale: Explicitly names quality, safety, and efficacy together as the three content domains whose information must all be transmitted under the same electronic standards framework.

rules.requirement: The objective is to define logical electronic communication standards for communication with Regulatory Authorities covering all information required in a submission and all associated information (primarily safety) required as part of the regulatory process.
rules.rationale: Scope covers all submission information (quality + efficacy content) and all associated information primarily safety. A single standard must carry all domain data.

rules.requirement: Logical communication standards should be independent of the detailed format and content of a submission, relating to information elements required in a submission, changing primarily in response to technology developments rather than content changes.
rules.rationale: Content-agnostic standard must be simultaneously applicable to quality module data, safety data, and efficacy data.

rules.requirement: Internationally agreed logical communication standards will enable safety updates to be provided instantaneously to many Authorities with a primary benefit to public health as international dissemination of product safety information will be rapid.
rules.rationale: Technical quality of electronic standards is a prerequisite for achieving safety outcomes within the regulatory framework.

rules.requirement: The Group will need expertise on both the information to be exchanged and technology standards. Input from E2 EWG (Clinical Safety Data) and M1 Medical Terminology group will be necessary.
rules.rationale: Mandatory cross-group input from E2 (clinical safety) and M1 (medical terminology) into M2 is a structural multidisciplinary governance requirement.

rules.requirement: Separate uncoordinated initiatives in different countries create potential for different standards adopted by different Authorities, leading to duplication of work. In the interests of public health this situation cannot continue.
rules.rationale: Fragmentation of electronic standards creates regulatory burden and risks delaying safety information dissemination.

rules.requirement: Proof of Concept Utilizing Generative AI: assessment report to include results from CBER HIVE Tool from M14 process comments, commercial AI tests, and Q6 AI test findings.
rules.rationale: AI proof-of-concept draws on M14 (efficacy/estimands) and Q6 (quality specifications) within M2 electronic data exchange context.

rules.requirement: M11 M2 collaboration: User Guide for ICH code from NCI and identify file format for downloading ICH code list for M11.
rules.rationale: M11 clinical protocols and M2 electronic standards collaboration spans terminology codes for adverse events, eligibility, endpoints, and regulatory format.

rules.requirement: Draft Business Case for ICH Terminology Harmonization with examples of challenges and M2 recommendations and options.
rules.rationale: Terminology harmonization across ICH guidelines affects quality terms, safety terms, efficacy terms, and regulatory terms.

rules.requirement: M2 Review of ICH Concept Papers for opportunities in harmonization of data exchange.
rules.rationale: Reviewing all ICH Concept Papers for data exchange harmonization requires assessment across Q, S, E, and M series.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: ICH M2 EWG has been mandated to define a process for the selection and management of terminology lists for the entire lifecycle of ICH work items, applying to terminology lists used in electronic information transfer (e.g. E2B message and M8 eCTD specifications).
rules.rationale: Single governance process must address terms originating in and affecting all four domains across their entire lifecycle.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: M2 EWG has produced processes to ensure all terminology lists used in ICH standards are managed through a single process pursuing consistent terminology for equivalent concepts across ICH topics.
rules.rationale: Most structurally comprehensive multidisciplinary requirement: same real-world concept must be expressed consistently whether it appears in quality, safety, efficacy, or regulatory context.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: Regional modifications to ICH terminology lists and purely regional terminology lists (e.g. Module 1 Context of Use terms for eCTD) are not covered by this document.
rules.rationale: Scoping out regional terms while covering harmonized core defines the boundary where multidisciplinary harmonization ends and regional variation begins.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: M2 EWG will contact ICH EWG/IWG during development phase and set up meetings to determine terminology list needs within first 6 months. M2 will also contact existing groups approaching Step 4.
rules.rationale: Procedural requirement for M2 to engage every new working group regardless of domain within defined timeframes creates structural multidisciplinary coordination.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: ICH M2 EWG has the responsibility to maintain the consistency and alignment of terminology lists used by ICH EWG/IWG groups.
rules.rationale: Standing cross-domain consistency obligation covering terminology in Q-series, S/E2-series, E-series, and M-series guidelines.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: M2 POC should ensure approved changes are consistent and aligned with usage within ICH, may need wider discussion, and may refer back to EWG/IWG if there is an issue.
rules.rationale: Change-control alignment means any proposed term change must be assessed for impact on all ICH guidelines using that term.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: When selecting externally maintained terminology lists: must identify version used at a certain time, see change history for a specific term, and determine status and date of status change.
rules.rationale: Version-locking and change-history traceability are simultaneously data quality, safety reporting reproducibility, and regulatory auditability requirements.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: Care should be taken with changes requesting values to be deleted - the list should retain the old term but identify it as no longer valid rather than deleting completely, ensuring people reviewing legacy data can still check validity.
rules.rationale: Prohibition on hard deletion preserves ability to audit historical safety reports, keeps regulatory archives interpretable, and maintains longitudinal data consistency.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: Preferred requirement is vocabulary governance where nothing is deleted, only deprecated with an alternative term provided, and notification when terms are deprecated.
rules.rationale: Deprecation-over-deletion governance has simultaneous data consistency, safety coding continuity, and audit trail preservation implications.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: When ICH uses externally maintained terminology lists, each ICH member party is responsible for its own refresh schedule. If refresh frequency is longer than the MO's update schedule, new terms may not be in the downloaded version.
rules.rationale: Stale terminology lists could cause adverse events to be uncoded (safety failure), create non-conforming submissions (regulatory failure), and introduce data quality errors.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: The IWG/EWG has responsibility for managing terminology list applicability and fitness for purpose. ICH M2 EWG provides technical expertise about terminology lists, development, maintenance, and liaising with MOs.
rules.rationale: Division of responsibility between domain EWGs (subject-matter fitness) and M2 EWG (technical expertise) creates structured multidisciplinary governance.

rules.source: ICH M2 Terminology List Management Processes v1_1.pdf
rules.requirement: If groups cannot reach mutually acceptable resolution on terminology issues, issues should be escalated to the ICH Management Committee.
rules.rationale: Escalation for unresolved terminology disputes reflects that terminology decisions have consequences across multiple domains requiring highest-level cross-domain governance authority.

rules.requirement: The ICH M7 Guideline recommendations provide a state-of-the-art approach for assessing the potential of impurities to induce point mutations and ensure that such impurities are controlled to safe levels so that below or above the ICH Q3A/B qualification threshold no further qualification for mutagenic potential is required. This includes the initial use of (Q)SAR tools to predict bacterial mutagenicity. In cases where the amount of the impurity exceeds 1 mg daily dose for chronic administration, evaluation of genotoxic potential as recommended in ICH Q3A/B could be considered. In cases where the amount of the impurity is equal to or less than 1 mg, no further genotoxicity testing is required regardless of other qualification thresholds.
rules.rationale: Directly links impurity qualification thresholds (quality), genotoxicity/mutagenicity assessment (safety), and regulatory submission requirements across all three domains simultaneously.

rules.requirement: To assess the mutagenic potential of impurities, a single bacterial mutagenicity assay can be carried out with a fully adequate protocol according to ICH S2(R1) and OECD 471 guidelines. The assays are expected to be performed in compliance with Good Laboratory Practices (GLP) regulations; however, lack of full GLP compliance does not necessarily mean that the data cannot be used to support clinical trials and marketing authorizations. Such deviations should be described in the study report.
rules.rationale: Links the safety testing standard (mutagenicity assay protocol per ICH S2(R1)), quality of study conduct (GLP compliance), and regulatory acceptability of data for marketing authorization — three domains in one requirement.

rules.requirement: For Option 4 approaches where justification based on scientific principles alone is not considered sufficient, as well as for Option 3 approaches, analytical data to support the control approach is expected. This could include as appropriate information on the structural changes to the impurity caused by downstream chemistry ('fate'), analytical data on pilot scale batches, and in some cases, laboratory scale studies with intentional addition of the impurity ('spiking studies'). In these cases, it is important to demonstrate that the fate/purge argument for the impurity is robust and will consistently assure a negligible probability of an impurity residing in the final drug substance above the acceptable limit.
rules.rationale: Requires chemistry/manufacturing process control data (quality), linked to the acceptable safety limit for a mutagenic impurity (safety), with a regulatory submission expectation (regulatory).

rules.requirement: The application of 'As Low As Reasonably Practicable' (ALARP) is not necessary if the level of the mutagenic impurity is below acceptable limits. Similarly, it is not necessary to demonstrate that alternate routes of synthesis have been explored. In cases where control efforts cannot reduce the level of the mutagenic impurity to below the acceptable limit and levels are ALARP, a higher limit may be justified based on a risk/benefit analysis.
rules.rationale: Bridges process chemistry control (quality domain), carcinogenic risk acceptability (safety domain), and the risk/benefit regulatory decision framework (regulatory domain).

rules.requirement: Allowance of Periodic Verification Testing is contingent upon use of a process that is under a state of control (i.e., produces a quality product that consistently meets specifications and conforms to an appropriately established facility, equipment, processing, and operational control regimen). If upon testing, the level of the mutagenic impurity fails to meet the acceptance criteria established for the periodic test, the drug producer should immediately commence full testing (i.e., testing of every batch for the attribute specified) until the cause of the failure has been conclusively determined, corrective action has been implemented, and the process is again documented to be in a state of control. As noted in ICH Q6A, regulatory authorities should be notified of a periodic verification test failure to evaluate the risk/benefit of previously released batches that were not tested.
rules.rationale: Directly joins quality system state-of-control requirements, safety (mutagenic impurity acceptance criteria), and regulatory notification obligations in a single integrated requirement.

rules.requirement: For a potential degradation product that has been characterized as mutagenic, it is important to understand if the degradation pathway is relevant to the drug substance and drug product manufacturing processes and/or their proposed packaging and storage conditions. A well-designed accelerated stability study (e.g., 40°C/75% relative humidity, 6 months) in the proposed packaging, with appropriate analytical procedures is recommended to determine the relevance of the potential degradation product. Based on the result of these accelerated studies, if it is anticipated that the degradation product will form at levels approaching the acceptable limit under the proposed packaging and storage conditions, then efforts to control formation of the degradation product is expected. In these cases, monitoring for the drug substance or drug product degradation product in long term primary stability studies at the proposed storage conditions (in the proposed commercial pack) is expected unless otherwise justified.
rules.rationale: Integrates stability testing (quality), mutagenic degradation product risk (safety), and packaging/storage specification requirements for marketing authorization (regulatory).

rules.requirement: If it is anticipated that formulation development and packaging design options are unable to control mutagenic degradation product levels to less than the acceptable limit and levels are as low as reasonably practicable, a higher limit can be justified based on a risk/benefit analysis.
rules.rationale: Bridges formulation/packaging design choices (quality), mutagenic safety limits (safety), and the regulatory risk/benefit justification mechanism.

rules.requirement: The quality system elements and management responsibilities described in ICH Q10 are intended to encourage the use of science-based and risk-based approaches at each lifecycle stage, thereby promoting continual improvement across the entire product lifecycle. Product and process knowledge should be managed from development through the commercial life of the product up to and including product discontinuation. Any proposed change to the manufacturing process should be evaluated for the impact on the quality of drug substance and drug product. This evaluation should be based on understanding of the manufacturing process and should determine if appropriate testing to analyze the impact of the proposed changes is required. Additionally, improvements in analytical procedures may lead to structural identification of an impurity. In those cases the new structure would be assessed for mutagenicity as described in this guideline.
rules.rationale: Links quality system lifecycle management (ICH Q10), mutagenicity re-assessment obligations triggered by manufacturing or analytical changes (safety), and regulatory filing requirements.

rules.requirement: All changes should be subject to internal change management processes as part of the quality system (ICH Q10). Changes to information filed and approved in a dossier should be reported to regulatory authorities in accordance with regional regulations and guidelines.
rules.rationale: Connects quality system change management requirements with regulatory reporting obligations for dossier changes, spanning both domains.

rules.requirement: It is recognized that product and process knowledge increases over the course of development and therefore it is expected that data to support control strategies in the clinical development trial phases will be less than at the marketing registration phase. A risk-based approach based on process chemistry fundamentals is encouraged to prioritize analytical efforts on those impurities with the highest likelihood of being present in the drug substance or drug product. Analytical data may not be expected to support early clinical development when the likelihood of an impurity being present is low, but in a similar situation analytical data may be appropriate to support the control approach for the marketing application. It is also recognized that commercial formulation design occurs later in clinical development and therefore efforts associated with drug product degradation products will be limited in the earlier phases.
rules.rationale: Spans clinical development phases (efficacy/clinical context), risk-based analytical control strategy (quality), impurity safety risk (safety), and staged regulatory submission expectations (regulatory).

rules.requirement: For Phase 1 studies of 14 days or less a description of efforts to mitigate risks of mutagenic impurities focused on Class 1, and Class 2 impurities and those in the cohort of concern as outlined in Section 7 should be included. For Phase 1 clinical trials greater than 14 days and for Phase 2a clinical trials additionally Class 3 impurities that require analytical controls should be included.
rules.rationale: Directly ties clinical trial duration (efficacy/clinical context) to impurity class safety requirements (safety) and analytical control expectations in regulatory submissions (quality/regulatory).

rules.requirement: For Phase 2b and Phase 3 clinical development trials, a list of the impurities assessed by (Q)SAR should be included, and any Class 1, 2 or 3 actual and potential impurities should be described along with plans for control. The in silico (Q)SAR systems used to perform the assessments should be described. The results of bacterial mutagenicity tests of actual impurities should be reported.
rules.rationale: Integrates computational hazard assessment (safety/quality), impurity control planning (quality), and regulatory documentation requirements across clinical development phases.

rules.requirement: For actual and potential process related impurities and degradation products where assessments according to this guideline are conducted, the mutagenic impurity classification and rationale for this classification should be provided. This would include the results and description of in silico (Q)SAR systems used, and as appropriate, supporting information to arrive at the overall conclusion for Class 4 and 5 impurities. When bacterial mutagenicity assays were performed on impurities, study reports should be provided for bacterial mutagenicity assays on impurities.
rules.rationale: Requires safety classification (safety), analytical/computational methodology documentation (quality), and formal regulatory submission content (regulatory) in an integrated manner.

rules.requirement: Justification for the proposed specification and the approach to control should be provided (e.g., ICH Q11 example 5b, Ref. 12). For example, this information could include the acceptable intake, the location and sensitivity of relevant routine monitoring. For Option 3 and Option 4 control approaches, a summary of knowledge of the purge factor, and identification of factors providing control (e.g., process steps, solubility in wash solutions, etc.) is important.
rules.rationale: Joins process chemistry purge justification (quality), acceptable intake safety limits (safety), and specification/regulatory filing requirements (regulatory).

rules.requirement: Throughout the lifecycle of the product, it will be important to reassess if testing is recommended when intended or unintended changes occur in the process. This applies when there is no routine monitoring at the acceptable limit (Option 3 or Option 4 control approaches), or when applying periodic rather than batch-by-batch testing. In some cases, the use of statistical process control and trending of process measurements can be useful for continued suitability and capability of processes to provide adequate control on the impurity. Statistical process control can be based on process parameters that influence impurity formation or clearance, even when that impurity is not routinely monitored (e.g., Option 4).
rules.rationale: Links process control and statistical monitoring (quality), to ensuring mutagenic impurity safety limits are maintained (safety), with lifecycle reassessment obligations (regulatory).

rules.requirement: Compound-specific risk assessments to derive acceptable intakes should be applied instead of the TTC-based (Threshold of Toxicological Concern-based) acceptable intakes where sufficient carcinogenicity data exist. For a known mutagenic carcinogen, a compound-specific acceptable intake can be calculated based on carcinogenic potency and linear extrapolation as a default approach. Alternatively, other established risk assessment practices such as those used by international regulatory bodies may be applied either to calculate acceptable intakes or to use already existing values published by regulatory authorities.
rules.rationale: Bridges toxicological risk assessment methodology (safety), the derivation of specification limits (quality), and the use of internationally recognised regulatory body values (regulatory).

rules.requirement: The existence of mechanisms leading to a dose response that is non-linear or has a practical threshold is increasingly recognized, not only for compounds that interact with non-DNA targets but also for DNA-reactive compounds, whose effects may be modulated by, for example, rapid detoxification before coming into contact with DNA, or by effective repair of induced damage. The regulatory approach to such compounds can be based on the identification of a No-Observed Effect Level (NOEL) and use of uncertainty factors (see ICH Q3C(R5)...) to calculate a Permissible Daily Exposure (PDE) when data are available.
rules.rationale: Integrates mechanistic toxicology for threshold modes of action (safety), PDE calculation methodology (quality/regulatory standard), and the regulatory framework for applying uncertainty factors consistent with residual solvent guidelines.

rules.requirement: Higher acceptable intakes may be justified when human exposure to the impurity will be much greater from other sources e.g., food, or endogenous metabolism (e.g., formaldehyde). The acceptable intake should be based on lifetime cancer risk of 1 in 100,000 as the default, applied at the acceptable daily intake level (1.5 micrograms/day TTC for a lifetime of treatment).
rules.rationale: Bridges endogenous/environmental exposure science (safety), the specification limit set in the drug substance (quality), and the regulatory risk level decision of 1:100,000 cancer risk.

rules.requirement: The above risk approaches described in Section 7 are applicable to all routes of administration and no corrections to acceptable intakes are generally warranted. Exceptions to consider may include situations where data justify route-specific concerns that should be evaluated case-by-case.
rules.rationale: Links route-of-administration pharmacology/toxicology (safety/efficacy), specification limit applicability (quality), and the regulatory expectation for case-by-case justification.

rules.requirement: It is emphasized that the AI or PDE values presented in this Addendum address carcinogenic risk. Other considerations, such as quality standards, may affect final product specifications. For example, the ICH M7 guidance (Section 7.2.2) notes that when calculating acceptable intakes from compound-specific risk assessments, an upper limit would be 0.5%, or, for example, 500 micrograms in a drug with a maximum daily dose of 100 mg.
rules.rationale: Explicitly states that the carcinogenic risk-derived AI (safety) must also be checked against quality specification limits (e.g., 0.5% cap), requiring both domains to be satisfied simultaneously for regulatory compliance.

rules.requirement: The assumptions for animal body weight, respiratory volume, and water consumption for calculation of doses were adopted from ICH Q3C and ICH Q3D.
rules.rationale: Explicitly cross-links the mutagenic impurity risk assessment methodology (safety) with the residual solvent and elemental impurity guideline calculation assumptions (quality/regulatory), making M7 calculations dependent on Q3C/Q3D parameters.

rules.requirement: The non-linear (threshold) mode of action approach: The regulatory approach to such compounds can be based on the identification of a No-Observed Effect Level (NOEL) and use of uncertainty factors (see ICH Q3C(R5)) to calculate a Permissible Daily Exposure (PDE) when data are available. Acceptable exposure levels for carcinogens with a threshold mode of action were established by calculation of PDEs. The PDE methodology is further explained in ICH Q3C(R5) and ICH Q3D.
rules.rationale: Requires mechanistic toxicological determination (safety), PDE calculation per Q3C(R5)/Q3D methodology (quality/analytical), and regulatory application of the threshold mode of action concept.

rules.requirement: Higher acceptable intakes may be justified when human exposure to the impurity will be much greater from other sources e.g., food or endogenous metabolism. As noted in ICH M7 Section 7.5: 'Higher acceptable intakes may be justified when human exposure to the impurity will be much greater from other sources e.g., food, or endogenous metabolism (e.g., formaldehyde).' For example, formaldehyde is not a carcinogen orally, so that regulatory limits have been based on non-cancer endpoints. Health Canada, WHO IPCS and US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) recommend an oral limit of 0.2 mg/kg/day, or 10 mg/day for a 50 kg person.
rules.rationale: Integrates endogenous/background exposure science (safety), the derivation of an appropriate specification limit (quality), and the adoption of international regulatory body limits (regulatory) in a single determination.

rules.requirement: For the Addendum AI calculation, the weight adjustment assumes an arbitrary adult human body weight for either sex of 50 kg. This relatively low weight provides an additional safety factor against the standard weights of 60 kg or 70 kg that are often used in this type of calculation. It is recognized that some adult patients weigh less than 50 kg; these patients are considered to be accommodated by the inherent conservatism (i.e., linear extrapolation of the most sensitive organ site) used to determine an AI.
rules.rationale: Links patient population safety considerations (safety), the numerical calculation methodology for specification limits (quality), and regulatory harmonization of the weight assumption across ICH guidelines.

rules.requirement: Formaldehyde AI (inhalation) = 8 mg/day or 215 ppb whichever is lower. The AI is derived by calculating the cumulative daily formaldehyde dose inhaled at the formaldehyde air concentration associated with 1:100,000 cancer risk. This cumulative daily dose is 8.2 mg/day, rounded to 8.0 mg/day and represents an upper limit over a 24 hour period. However, inhaling 8 mg formaldehyde with one breath e.g., with an inhalation drug taken once per day via an inhaler, is not considered appropriate. Therefore the limit of 215 ppb is also applied.
rules.rationale: Bridges the carcinogenic risk quantification (safety), the specific product format/formulation consideration for inhalation drug products (quality), and the dual-limit regulatory specification approach.

rules.requirement: Q&A 1.2: In the context of ICH M7, (Quantitative) Structure-Activity Relationships ((Q)SAR) is considered an appropriate initial evaluation of mutagenic potential of an impurity at a daily dose of less than or equal to 1 mg. When a structural alert is identified, a follow-up in vitro evaluation (e.g., bacterial reverse mutation assay) could be conducted, or the impurity could be controlled by Threshold of Toxicological Concern (TTC). Negative results in either evaluation would classify the impurity under Class 5. The result of the bacterial reverse mutation assay overrules the (Q)SAR prediction. Additionally, impurities should not be assigned to Class 5 based solely on the absence of structural alerts by visual evaluation alone. There is an expectation that structural alert assessment will be conducted using (Q)SAR prediction.
rules.rationale: Spans computational hazard assessment methodology (safety/quality), the TTC control threshold (quality/safety), and the regulatory classification system (Class 1-5) for impurities.

rules.requirement: Q&A 1.3: In cases where the amount of impurity is >1 mg daily dose for chronic administration, and an impurity generated negative predictions in two appropriate (Q)SAR systems, a minimum screen of genotoxicity studies (point mutation and chromosomal aberration) could be considered. The 1 mg refers to an absolute amount of an impurity, irrespective of the identification or qualification thresholds outlined in the ICH Q3A/B guidelines.
rules.rationale: Connects ICH Q3A/B qualification thresholds (quality), the genotoxicity testing requirement triggered by dose level (safety), and the independent regulatory classification under M7 regardless of Q3 thresholds.

rules.requirement: Q&A 2.1: If a semi-synthetic drug substance, as defined in ICH Q11, is manufactured using steps that could introduce mutagenic impurities or degradation products (e.g., post-modification of a fermentation product or late-stage introduction of a linker) a risk assessment is warranted. The following compounds used in the manufacturing process of semi-synthetic drug substances and drug products should be considered within the scope of the application of ICH M7: chemically-synthesized intermediates and actual impurities therein; reagents.
rules.rationale: Extends M7 scope (regulatory) into semi-synthetic manufacturing (quality), to address mutagenic safety risk (safety), using the ICH Q11 definition as the cross-reference trigger.

rules.requirement: Q&A 3.1: Non-mutagenic carcinogens (i.e., carcinogens that are negative in the bacterial reverse mutation assay) do not have a DNA reactive mechanism of carcinogenicity and therefore are not in scope of the ICH M7 guideline (e.g., acetamide and hydroxylamine). Q&A 3.2: Mutagens that are demonstrated to be non-carcinogenic in appropriate and well-conducted animal bioassays will be classified as Class 5 impurities.
rules.rationale: Defines the boundary between M7 (mutagenic carcinogens) and other regulatory frameworks (Q3A/B for non-mutagenic carcinogens), requiring cross-domain classification decisions affecting which safety control framework applies and what quality specifications are set.

rules.requirement: Q&A 4.1: Any increase in dose of the active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) that would increase any mutagenic impurity to levels above the acceptable limits is considered significant (see Tables 2 and 3 and the addendum of ICH M7). In such cases a re-evaluation of the mutagenic impurity limits is recommended.
rules.rationale: Directly connects dose changes driven by clinical efficacy needs (efficacy) to mutagenic impurity safety limits (safety) and the consequent requirement to revise specifications and regulatory filings (quality/regulatory).

rules.requirement: Q&A 6.1: (Q)SAR models utilized for ICH M7 purposes should follow OECD Principles of (Q)SAR Validation: (1) A defined endpoint trained using standard OECD protocol for in vitro Bacterial Reverse Mutation Assay; (2) An unambiguous algorithm; (3) A defined domain of applicability; (4) Appropriate measures of goodness-of-fit, robustness and predictivity; (5) A mechanistic interpretation. For any system as minimum requirement to demonstrate how each model follows these principles, the sponsor is expected to provide the OECD (Q)SAR Model Reporting Format (QMRF) on request by the regulatory agency.
rules.rationale: Mandates specific computational model validation standards (quality/analytical), linked to mutagenicity prediction for safety assessment (safety), with formal regulatory submission documentation requirements (regulatory).

rules.requirement: Q&A 6.2: An out of domain or non-coverage result from one of the two (Q)SAR models requires additional assessment to classify the compound as a Class 5 impurity. Expert review may include one or a combination of: (1) Comparison to structurally similar analogs for which bacterial reverse mutation assay data are available (read-across approach); (2) Expert review of the chemical structure to determine if there is potential for the chemical to react with DNA; (3) (Q)SAR output from an additional validated model of the same methodology that generates a prediction within its applicability domain.
rules.rationale: Links computational model applicability (quality/analytical), chemical structure-DNA reactivity science (safety), and the regulatory classification outcome for impurity control strategy decisions.

rules.requirement: Q&A 6.3: If an impurity tests negative in an Ames assay, it is considered a Class 5 impurity. Addressing positive results in a clastogenicity assay is out of scope of ICH M7.
rules.rationale: Establishes the boundary of safety test type (Ames/mutagenicity vs. clastogenicity) for regulatory impurity classification, directly affecting what quality specification and control strategy must be applied.

rules.requirement: Q&A 6.4: If an impurity is positive in the Ames test, and levels of the impurity cannot be controlled to an appropriate acceptable limit, an in vivo follow-up test with mutagenic endpoint (mutagenicity) should be used. The other follow-up tests outlined in Note 3 are also acceptable when scientific rationale is provided to support their use. For any of the above tests, adequate exposure should be demonstrated in line with ICH S2.
rules.rationale: Connects analytical control strategy failure (quality), the requirement for in vivo safety follow-up testing with adequate exposure (safety), and the regulatory acceptability of those tests per ICH S2.

rules.requirement: Q&A 7.1: A well conducted and scientifically justified in vivo study (see Q&A 6.4) is sufficient to demonstrate lack of in vivo relevance. If the results of the in vivo study are negative, the impurity can be assigned to ICH M7 Class 5.
rules.rationale: Bridges in vivo safety study outcome (safety) with regulatory impurity classification (Class 5, regulatory) that eliminates the need for a specific quality specification/control in the drug substance.

rules.requirement: Q&A 7.2: When a mutagenic impurity cannot be controlled to the TTC (or less than lifetime-based limit), results from an appropriate in vivo assay could complement the available data for a weight of evidence approach to support a higher limit on a case by case basis. However, in vivo gene mutation assays alone are currently not validated to directly assess cancer risk because the endpoint is mutation and not carcinogenicity (i.e., they are used for hazard identification).
rules.rationale: Integrates in vivo mutagenicity testing (safety), the inability to control impurity levels to specification (quality), and the regulatory weight-of-evidence approach to justify a higher limit — all three domains in a single decision framework.

rules.requirement: Q&A 7.3: The LTL (less-than-lifetime) approach can be applied to compounds with exposure limits based on the TTC or a compound/class specific AI. However, this approach is not applicable to PDEs as linearity of dose duration response is not considered sufficiently demonstrated for threshold related mechanisms. Higher levels of exposure for short-term exposure (30 days or less) may be acceptable on a case by case basis.
rules.rationale: Bridges exposure duration pharmacology (safety/efficacy context), the calculation methodology for specification limits (quality), and the regulatory framework for when higher limits are acceptable.

rules.requirement: Q&A 7.4: The treatment duration category for HIV disease was changed from 1-10 years to >10 years - lifetime because of advances in the clinical treatment of HIV disease. To avoid disruption of supply of HIV drugs already on the market, this change will not be applied to currently marketed products. For HIV treatment-related regulatory submissions 18 months after the date that the M7(R2) reached Step 4, the 1.5 micrograms/day or other appropriate acceptable intake will be applied in the following situations: new drug substances and new drug products during their clinical development and subsequent applications for marketing; changes to the drug substance synthesis resulting in new impurities or increased acceptance criteria for existing impurities; changes in the formulation, composition or manufacturing process resulting in new degradation products or increased acceptance criteria for existing degradation products; introduction of a new source of the drug substance through a drug master file (DMF).
rules.rationale: Integrates clinical therapeutic advances for HIV (efficacy), the consequent recalculation of acceptable mutagenic impurity intake (safety), the impact on drug substance specifications and control (quality), and the staged regulatory implementation timeline (regulatory).

rules.requirement: Q&A 7.5: When three or more Class 2 or Class 3 impurities are specified in the drug substance specification, a limit for each 'Individual Impurity' should be listed in the drug substance specification as per limits provided in Table 2 (for example >10 years to lifetime NMT 1.5 micrograms/day). Additionally, a limit for 'Total Mutagenic Impurities' should be listed in the drug substance specification as per limits provided in Table 3. Compound-specific or class-related acceptable limits (Class 1) and degradation products which form in the drug product are excluded from total mutagenic impurity limits.
rules.rationale: Combines the safety rationale for individual and cumulative impurity limits (safety), the technical specification-writing requirement (quality), and the regulatory expectation for how limits are presented in submissions.

rules.requirement: Q&A 8.1: Use of Option 4 is appropriate when a mutagenic impurity has a negligible risk of being present in the final drug substance. The risk can be considered negligible if predictive purge calculations based on scientific principles (e.g., impurity reactivity or solubility) result in impurity levels <1% of TTC or AI. When predictive purge calculations result in impurity levels ≥1% of TTC or AI, measured purge factors (i.e., spike and purge data) showing impurity levels less than 10% of TTC or AI should be provided to justify Option 4 control. The process-relevant conditions should be considered for the purge calculation and the generation of analytical data.
rules.rationale: Bridges process chemistry purge science (quality), the numerical safety threshold criteria (safety), and the regulatory justification framework for eliminating a specification test from a drug substance.

rules.requirement: Q&A 8.2: Predictive purge calculations should be based on the drug substance manufacturing process as described in the application and should consider reactivity, solubility, volatility, and other factors of the impurity in each step. The predictive purge calculation should use conservative values and methodology, since predictive purge often does not rely on experimental confirmation. The amount of information (i.e., impurity reactivity or solubility data, spike and purge data under the process relevant conditions) to justify a predictive purge calculation approach should be guided by knowledge of the manufacturing process, risk to the final drug substance, and the stage of drug development.
rules.rationale: Integrates process chemistry science (quality), the risk-to-patient from mutagenic impurity presence (safety), and the regulatory expectation for documentation level commensurate with development stage and risk.

rules.requirement: Q&A 8.3: For mutagenic impurities introduced or generated in the last synthetic step, given the proximity to the final product, Option 1 control approach is preferred. However, Option 2 and 3 control approaches may be possible when appropriately justified. The control strategy may be influenced by the presence of a subsequent recrystallization step, a highly effective purification operation (e.g., chromatography or well-defined crystallization), the reactivity (e.g., highly reactive reagents such as thionyl chloride) and physical characteristics of the impurity (e.g., low boiling point such as methyl chloride). In most cases, for mutagenic impurities introduced or generated in the last synthetic step, the justification of an Option 4 control approach solely based on prediction is not sufficient and supporting analytical data should be provided.
rules.rationale: Combines synthesis process proximity and purification effectiveness (quality), the mutagenic risk requiring conservative control (safety), and the regulatory expectation for analytical data supporting the chosen control option.

rules.requirement: Q&A 8.4: Periodic verification testing (i.e., skip testing) is not appropriate for Option 2 and 3 control approaches. In section 8.1 of ICH M7, periodic verification testing is only discussed as a control strategy when using Option 1 control approach. The Option 1 periodic verification testing strategy references ICH Q6A. The Option 1 periodic verification testing concept (per ICH Q6A) should generally be implemented post-approval and applies to testing in the final drug substance.
rules.rationale: Links the quality concept of skip testing from ICH Q6A to the safety context of mutagenic impurity control, with regulatory restrictions on when this approach is permissible — spanning all three domains.

rules.requirement: Q&A 8.6: Lab scale experiments are typically sufficient when generating measured purge factors or when defining in-process control points. These studies should employ conditions representative of the final process as described in the application and should consider the potential impact of scale and equipment related differences between the laboratory and production environment. In the case of observed scale dependencies, confirmatory testing on batches manufactured at pilot or commercial scale may be advisable.
rules.rationale: Bridges manufacturing scale-up science (quality), the need to ensure mutagenic impurity purge data is representative of commercial production (safety), and regulatory submission requirements for scale representativeness.

rules.requirement: Q&A 9.1: A Sponsor is not expected to update their (Q)SAR assessment during drug development unless there is a safety concern such as when newly available bacterial reverse mutagenicity assay data and/or mechanistic knowledge suggest that the prediction is incorrect. It is recommended that the sponsor re-run (Q)SAR predictions prior to the initial marketing application to ensure predictions reflect the most current data available. In general, predictions generated with models developed prior to ICH M7's publication in 2014 are considered unacceptable.
rules.rationale: Links computational model currency (quality/analytical), safety reassessment triggers (safety), and regulatory submission expectations across the development lifecycle.

rules.requirement: Q&A 9.2: In Module 2, a brief summary of the ICH M7 risk assessment and control strategy should be included (sections 2.3 and 2.6). In Module 3, the ICH M7 risk assessment and control strategy should be provided in detail per ICH M4Q guidance. In Module 4, full safety study-related information on impurities (e.g., bacterial reverse mutagenicity assay reports, (Q)SAR reports, other genotoxicity test reports, additional testing, etc.) should be included to support the risk assessment and control strategy.
rules.rationale: Explicitly requires safety study data (Module 4/safety domain), control strategy (Module 3/quality domain), and summary risk assessment (Module 2/regulatory domain) to be cross-referenced and integrated in the CTD regulatory submission.

rules.requirement: The ICH M7 Addendum on N-Nitrosamine Impurities will include general principles and approaches for establishing acceptable intakes for nitrosamines, as well as a framework for applying quality principles provided in the ICH M7 Guideline and its Q&A for their assessment and control. The Addendum will include recommendations for safety and quality topics to support consistency across ICH regions, including: Recommendations for the design and interpretation of the EAT to assess mutagenic hazard; Recommendations for defining acceptable intakes based on physicochemical and structural features of a nitrosamine; Application of less-than-lifetime (LTL) adjustments to acceptable intakes based on exposure duration; Control of multiple nitrosamines within a drug substance and/or drug product; Framework for using quality principles recommended in the ICH M7(R2) guideline and its Q&A.
rules.rationale: Proposes an entire new framework that explicitly bridges safety hazard assessment (EAT, mutagenic hazard), quality control strategies (control options 1-4, multiple impurities), and regulatory harmonization across ICH regions for N-nitrosamine impurities.

rules.requirement: The Carcinogenic Potency Categorisation Approach (CPCA) was established in 2023 to assign a nitrosamine to one of five predicted carcinogenic potency categories, each with a corresponding acceptable intake that ranges from 18 ng/day up to 1500 ng/day. However, the recommended acceptable intake in the case where a nitrosamine does not exhibit a mutagenic hazard (for example, EAT and/or in vivo mutagenicity study is negative), is not aligned across ICH regions.
rules.rationale: Highlights a cross-domain gap where safety hazard determination (mutagenicity outcome) is not harmonized with acceptable intake specification limits (quality) across different regulatory regions (regulatory), requiring multidisciplinary resolution.

rules.requirement: Potential to develop a weight of evidence approach based on the outcome of other in vitro mutagenicity assays (e.g., in vitro assays described in ICH S2(R1)), metabolism/activation data, and in vivo studies (e.g., transgenic rodent assay), as warranted for establishing an AI. Principles for the design and interpretation of in vivo mutation studies, and whether and how the generated data may be used for derivation of acceptable intakes (including quantitative assessment of in vivo data when the results show evidence of mutagenicity).
rules.rationale: Links the design and interpretation of safety studies (safety), their use for deriving numerical specification limits (quality), and the regulatory framework for using in vivo data for AI derivation.

rules.requirement: Recommended principles for conducting robust quality risk assessments for drug products, drug substances, and other drug product components, including high level guidance concerning risk factors for nitrosamines. Recommendations for control strategies for drug substances and extension of control strategies to drug products. This can include options 1-4. High level guidance on documentation in CTDs.
rules.rationale: Explicitly combines quality risk assessment principles, safety-driven control strategy options, and regulatory CTD documentation guidance into a single integrated framework for nitrosamine impurities.

rules.requirement: The M7(R2) EWG is to update the M7 text and develop a Question and Answer document to clarify and address Quality and Safety issues and concerns that have been identified from experience through implementation of M7 since its publication in 2014. Topics to be discussed are additional clarification on the justification of control strategy for mutagenic impurities in the marketing authorization dossier, organization and depth of information reporting of individual mutagenic impurities, (Q)SAR systems, and other safety-related information. The focus of the EWG will encompass safety assessment by toxicologists and quality assessment by chemists.
rules.rationale: Explicitly states the EWG mandate is multidisciplinary, with toxicologists (safety) and chemists (quality) jointly addressing regulatory dossier requirements, demonstrating the intrinsic cross-domain nature of the M7 framework.

rules.requirement: EU proposed the change of treatment duration for HIV medicines in Examples of clinical use scenarios for applying acceptable intakes (Note 7, Table 4). Life expectancy of HIV patients under HRT is close to normal. Treatments have improved, and high treatment compliance has diminished resistance development. Frequent changes of anti-retroviral medicines are not any more done periodically. Therefore, HIV medicines should be classified as medicines for life long treatment.
rules.rationale: Clinical therapeutic advancement (efficacy domain) directly drives a change in the acceptable intake limit for mutagenic impurities (safety), which then requires specification updates (quality) and regulatory implementation procedures (regulatory).

rules.source: Annex 4 - Maintenance Procedure for Q3C, Q3D, and M7.pdf
rules.requirement: The M7 Addendum Maintenance Procedure applies to: Incorporation of acceptable limits (Acceptable Intakes (AIs) or PDEs) for new DNA reactive (mutagenic) impurities and revising acceptable limits for impurities already listed in the Addendum as new data becomes available. Information provided within a proposal should be based on significant toxicity data from studies such as repeat-dose studies, reproductive toxicity studies, genotoxicity studies, and carcinogenicity studies and/or other relevant studies. Single-dose toxicity data alone are not sufficient. The toxicity data should be of sufficient quality to calculate a PDE or AI. Genotoxicity and carcinogenicity data are of primary importance for revisions to the M7 Guideline.
rules.rationale: The maintenance procedure itself is inherently multidisciplinary: it requires safety data (repeat-dose, carcinogenicity, genotoxicity), of sufficient quality for PDE/AI calculation (quality), to revise regulatory specification limits in the Addendum. It also links M7 to Q3C and Q3D through a shared governance procedure.

rules.source: Annex 4 - Maintenance Procedure for Q3C, Q3D, and M7.pdf
rules.requirement: A revision will be considered only on presentation of new data or previously un-recognised toxicity data sufficient to result in a significant change, or because of convincing evidence that the existing data used to calculate a PDE are invalid. When a new or revised PDE or AI is recommended by the EWG, approval by the ICH MC is required. Once approval occurs, the information should be disseminated as quickly as possible to all ICH participants and other members of the chemical and pharmaceutical communities.
rules.rationale: Links new toxicological evidence (safety), its impact on calculated PDE/AI values used in specifications (quality), and the regulatory governance process required to update harmonized limits across all ICH regions.

rules.requirement: Note 3 — Tests to Investigate the in vivo Relevance of in vitro Mutagens (Positive Bacterial Mutagenicity): The selection of an in vivo follow-up test should be fit-for-purpose, with options including Transgenic mutation assays (for any bacterial mutagenicity positive; justify selection of assay tissue/organ); Pig-a assay (blood, for directly acting mutagens positive without S9); Micronucleus test (blood or bone marrow, for directly acting mutagens and compounds known to be clastogenic); Rat liver Unscheduled DNA Synthesis (UDS) test (in particular for bacterial mutagenicity positive with S9 only); Comet assay (justification needed for chemical class specific mode of action). For indirect acting mutagens (requiring metabolic activation), adequate exposure to metabolite(s) should be demonstrated.
rules.rationale: Bridges the mechanistic toxicology of different mutagen types (safety), the choice of fit-for-purpose in vivo test (quality of study design), and the regulatory acceptance criteria for each test type in the M7 framework.

rules.requirement: Note 4 — Example of linear extrapolation from the TD50: It is possible to calculate a compound-specific acceptable intake based on rodent carcinogenicity potency data such as TD50 values (doses giving a 50% tumor incidence equivalent to a cancer risk probability level of 1:2). Linear extrapolation to a probability of 1 in 100,000 (i.e., the accepted lifetime risk level used) is achieved by simply dividing the TD50 by 50,000. This procedure is similar to that employed for derivation of the TTC. AI = TD50/50,000 x 50 kg.
rules.rationale: Defines the quantitative bridge between carcinogenicity data (safety science), the mathematical derivation of a specification limit in micrograms per day (quality), and the regulatory risk level of 1:100,000 lifetime cancer probability.

rules.requirement: The 2nd Addendum was developed to include additional monographs for 7 mutagenic impurities and derive Acceptable Intakes (AIs) for them. Additionally an update was made in the main M7 guideline text changing the HIV treatment duration from 1-10 years to >10 years - lifetime.
rules.rationale: The HIV treatment duration update is driven by clinical efficacy advances, which directly changes the acceptable intake (safety limit), which in turn changes drug substance specifications (quality) and requires regulatory implementation.

rules.requirement: Acceptable exposure levels for carcinogens with a threshold mode of action were established by calculation of PDEs. Chemicals that induce methemoglobinemia, hemosiderin deposits in tissues such as spleen, and subsequent inflammation and tumors (e.g., aniline and related compounds) support that mutagenicity was not central to the mode of action. Chemicals that induce tumors associated with local irritation/inflammation (such as rodent forestomach tumors) and are site-of-contact carcinogens may be considered not relevant to human exposure at low, non-irritating concentrations as potential impurities in pharmaceuticals (e.g., benzyl chloride). Chemicals that act through oxidative damage, so that deleterious effects do not occur at lower doses since abundant endogenous protective mechanisms exist, (e.g., hydrogen peroxide).
rules.rationale: Mode of action determination (safety science) directly governs whether a PDE or AI is set and what the numerical specification limit will be (quality), with different regulatory justification required for each mechanistic class.

rules.requirement: The ICH M7 Nitrosamine Sub-Group Work Plan includes: Data gathering/analyses/discussions related to design and interpretation of the enhanced Ames test (EAT) to assess mutagenic hazard; Benchmark dose (BMD) methodology education to analyze carcinogenicity data and support the derivation of Acceptable Intakes; Structural feature prevalence analysis — Analysis completed to inform discussions regarding the value of incorporating additional structural features into the carcinogenic potency categorization approach (CPCA) framework; Data gathering/analyses/discussions related to less-than-lifetime (LTL) framework and in vivo mutation study design and interpretation.
rules.rationale: The entire work plan simultaneously addresses safety testing design (EAT, in vivo mutation studies), analytical/computational methodology for deriving specification limits (BMD, CPCA), and the regulatory framework harmonization (LTL adjustments, CPCA categories with corresponding AIs).

rules.requirement: ICH M7(R1) was issued as Step 4 and contains Appendix 3 (Application of the Principles of the ICH M7 Guideline to Calculation of Compound-Specific Acceptable Intakes). The concept paper of ICH-M7(R2) includes maintenance of ICH-M7 with the 2nd addendum (application of the Principles of the ICH M7 Guideline to Calculation of Compound-Specific Acceptable Intakes) and Q&A topics proposed based on experience through implementation of ICH-M7. M7(R2) Guideline and Addendum reached Step 4 of ICH process in April 2023.
rules.rationale: Documents the evolution of the multidisciplinary M7 framework through successive revisions, each combining compound-specific safety limits (safety), their derivation methodology (quality), and formal regulatory harmonization milestones.

rules.requirement: The eCTD structure must organise quality (Module 3), nonclinical safety (Module 4), and clinical efficacy/safety (Module 5) data into a single harmonised electronic submission that regulators can navigate as an integrated dossier.
rules.rationale: The eCTD format itself is the cross-domain artefact binding quality, nonclinical, and clinical data.

rules.requirement: Regional eCTD implementation specifications must be harmonised so the same electronic dossier containing quality, safety, and efficacy data can be submitted simultaneously to ICH member regulators without reformatting.
rules.rationale: Harmonisation ensures the integrated quality-safety-efficacy narrative is preserved identically across jurisdictions.

rules.requirement: M8 EWG shall revise eCTD specifications to accommodate new modalities (gene therapy, cell therapy, mRNA products) whose quality, nonclinical safety, and clinical data sets have structural requirements not addressed in current module architecture.
rules.rationale: Emerging modalities generate data spanning quality, safety, and efficacy within the same module sections.

rules.requirement: A BCS-based biowaiver guideline shall bridge the quality domain (in vitro dissolution testing) with the efficacy/clinical domain (in vivo bioequivalence demonstration), establishing conditions under which in vitro data can substitute for a clinical BA/BE study.
rules.rationale: Foundational premise of M9: dissolution (quality measurement) accepted as surrogate for bioequivalence (clinical efficacy measurement).

rules.requirement: M9 guideline development shall demonstrate that granting a biowaiver on the basis of BCS classification and in vitro dissolution does not compromise patient safety or therapeutic efficacy.
rules.rationale: Biowaiver eligibility is framed as a patient-safety and efficacy question, not merely a quality question.

rules.requirement: A drug substance shall be classified as highly soluble only when the highest dose strength is soluble in 250 mL or less of aqueous media over pH 1.2 to 6.8 at 37 degrees C.
rules.rationale: Solubility is physicochemical/quality but 250 mL threshold is set based on in vivo GI fluid volume at clinical dose.

rules.requirement: A drug substance shall be classified as highly permeable when extent of intestinal absorption in humans is 85% or more, demonstrated by mass balance, absolute bioavailability, or validated in vitro permeability methods correlated with in vivo data.
rules.rationale: In vitro permeability (quality) acceptable only if validated against human clinical absorption data (efficacy).

rules.requirement: For BCS Class I products, both test and reference must release 85% or more of labelled amount within 15 minutes in pH 1.2, 4.5, and 6.8 buffers to replace an in vivo BE study.
rules.rationale: 15-minute 85% threshold is a quality dissolution specification calibrated to clinical gastric emptying rate.

rules.requirement: For BCS Class III products, the test formulation must contain the same excipients as reference; where differences exist, it must be demonstrated they do not affect the rate or extent of drug absorption in vivo.
rules.rationale: Excipient composition (quality) is gated on in vivo absorption (efficacy) and potential toxicological effects of excipient-mediated transport changes (safety).

rules.requirement: Narrow therapeutic index (NTI) drugs are not eligible for BCS-based biowaivers regardless of BCS classification; in vivo bioequivalence studies must be conducted.
rules.rationale: NTI exclusion is a patient-safety rule applied to a quality/formulation decision: the small margin between efficacious and toxic doses means in vitro methods cannot detect clinically relevant absorption differences.

rules.requirement: When in vitro dissolution similarity cannot be demonstrated by f2 factor, additional dissolution tests and/or in vivo studies may be required, integrating quality dissolution data with clinical bioequivalence risk assessment.
rules.rationale: f2 similarity factor (quality metric) directly linked to in vivo bioequivalence risk (efficacy metric); ambiguous quality data escalates to clinical evidence.

rules.requirement: Documentation for BCS-based biowaiver must include drug substance solubility data, permeability data with method validation, dissolution data for test and reference products, and clinical justification for why in vitro evidence is sufficient.
rules.rationale: Documentation mandates integrated quality-efficacy submission: applicant must bridge physicochemical measurements to in vivo therapeutic equivalence expectations.

rules.requirement: In vitro permeability methods must be validated using compounds with known in vivo human intestinal permeability; high-permeability boundary set using compounds with 85% or more human absorption as calibrators.
rules.rationale: Validation of quality lab method is anchored to human clinical absorption data creating mandatory quality-efficacy validation link.

rules.requirement: NTI drugs are excluded from BCS-based biowaivers because the narrow safety margin between minimum effective and minimum toxic concentrations means formulation-related absorption variability undetectable by in vitro methods could result in therapeutic failure or toxicity.
rules.rationale: Connects quality limitation of in vitro dissolution to simultaneous safety (toxicity) and efficacy (therapeutic failure) consequences.

rules.requirement: Fixed-dose combination (FDC) products may be eligible for biowaivers if each active ingredient individually meets BCS criteria; eligibility must be assessed component by component with each component's safety and efficacy profile evaluated independently.
rules.rationale: FDC biowaiver requires simultaneous quality assessment, safety evaluation, and efficacy verification per API in a single product decision.

rules.requirement: When Caco-2 or cell-based permeability assays are used, applicants must demonstrate the drug is not a substrate for efflux transporters (e.g. P-gp, BCRP); if active transport is involved, in vivo clinical data are required.
rules.rationale: Transporter-mediated efflux is simultaneously a quality analytical limitation, a pharmacokinetic/efficacy concern, and a safety concern via DDI risk.

rules.requirement: Salt forms may qualify for biowaiver assessment if solubility and permeability criteria are met, accounting for potential in vivo conversion and resulting pH-dependent absorption profile.
rules.rationale: Salt form selection (quality/chemistry) biowaiver eligibility governed by in vivo pharmacokinetic consequences of salt-to-base conversion.

rules.requirement: Excipient grade (pharmaceutical vs. food grade) may be considered for Class III products if there is evidence the grade difference affects excipient quantity or purity influencing in vivo absorption.
rules.rationale: Excipient grade (quality specification) evaluated against in vivo absorption impact (efficacy).

rules.requirement: This multidisciplinary guideline addresses BCS-based waivers of bioequivalence studies; applicants and regulators must apply it understanding that quality (dissolution), safety (NTI exclusion, excipient toxicity), and efficacy (bioequivalence) considerations are inseparable.
rules.rationale: ICH itself explicitly labels M9 as multidisciplinary, framing the entire biowaiver framework as a joint quality-safety-efficacy determination.

rules.requirement: The new multidisciplinary guideline will apply to validation of bioanalytical methods and study sample analyses in non-clinical and clinical studies, addressing method validation, study sample analysis, and ISR requirements.
rules.rationale: M10 explicitly designated multidisciplinary: bioanalytical methods underpin nonclinical safety studies and clinical PK/PD studies, making validation inherently cross-domain.

rules.requirement: Harmonised bioanalytical method validation standards shall contribute to appropriate dose finding and evaluation of safety and efficacy by ensuring concentration-time data underpinning PK/PD and TK assessments are generated by methods of demonstrated reliability.
rules.rationale: Method validation quality contributes to safety (TK for toxicology) and efficacy (PK/PD for dose finding).

rules.requirement: Bioanalytical methods supporting nonclinical safety studies under GLP and clinical studies under GCP must be validated according to M10, with different acceptance criteria for chromatographic methods and ligand binding assays.
rules.rationale: Single validation framework governs methods for safety studies (GLP) and efficacy/clinical studies (GCP).

rules.requirement: Selectivity must be assessed using minimum six individual matrix sources; for special populations (hepatic/renal impairment, paediatric, elderly), selectivity must be re-evaluated in matrix from those populations.
rules.rationale: Selectivity (quality/analytical) must be extended to patient-specific matrices relevant to clinical safety and efficacy evaluation.

rules.requirement: Specificity must evaluate potential back-conversion of metabolites to parent drug and interference of co-administered drugs; when back-conversion is detected, impact on PK parameters (Cmax, AUC) used for safety and efficacy conclusions must be assessed.
rules.rationale: Metabolite back-conversion is analytical quality problem with regulatory consequence of distorting PK parameters (efficacy) and TK parameters (safety).

rules.requirement: Matrix effect must be evaluated using matrix from at least six donors; for LBA methods, endogenous interferents specific to patient population must be tested; bias exceeding 15% from nominal is unacceptable.
rules.rationale: Matrix effect is quality problem but requirement to test patient-population-specific matrix links to clinical and nonclinical data quality.

rules.requirement: LLOQ must be set with accuracy within 20% and precision 20% CV or less; LLOQ must be appropriate to measure concentrations at least three times the terminal elimination half-life post-dose.
rules.rationale: LLOQ is quality parameter but adequacy defined by PK requirements of the study for TK (safety) and PK/PD (efficacy).

rules.requirement: ISR is required for all pivotal nonclinical safety studies and clinical studies; at least 10% of study samples (minimum 20) must be reanalysed with 67% agreement within 20% of the mean.
rules.rationale: ISR is quality/reproducibility measure mandated for both safety and clinical studies, with failures triggering investigation of all regulatory data.

rules.requirement: Analyte stability must be evaluated under all conditions of sample collection, processing, and storage used in actual study; stability failures invalidate study sample data used for both safety and efficacy conclusions.
rules.rationale: Stability assessment is quality procedure but analyte degradation directly corrupts TK exposure data (safety) and PK/PD data (efficacy).

rules.requirement: Carry-over must be assessed; carry-over in blank must not exceed 20% of LLOQ response; unacceptable carry-over invalidates low-concentration quantification critical for terminal elimination characterisation in TK and PK studies.
rules.rationale: Carry-over is quality artefact impacting AUC and half-life calculations underpinning safety exposure margins and efficacy dose-response.

rules.requirement: Cross-validation is required when multiple methods or laboratories generate data for the same study or across studies supporting a single regulatory decision; must demonstrate data interchangeability for PK/TK conclusions.
rules.rationale: Cross-validation is quality process triggered by combined data use for safety or efficacy decisions.

rules.requirement: For endogenous analytes (biomarkers, endogenous peptides), surrogate matrix or surrogate analyte approach must be validated demonstrating the surrogate faithfully represents the endogenous analyte behaviour in the biological matrix.
rules.rationale: Surrogate matrix selection (quality) is judged against ability to accurately measure biomarkers used for safety monitoring and efficacy assessment.

rules.requirement: Calibration curves must include minimum six non-zero standards; at least 75% of calibrators must meet accuracy criteria (15% of nominal, 20% at LLOQ); failed curves must be rejected affecting PK and TK profile completeness.
rules.rationale: Calibration curve acceptance (quality) creates gaps in exposure-time profiles for TK (safety) and PK (efficacy) when rejected.

rules.requirement: For LBA, hook effect must be assessed; samples with high analyte concentrations must be diluted to verify quantification falls within validated range; failure leads to false-low reporting affecting safety and efficacy interpretation.
rules.rationale: Hook effect is LBA-specific quality artefact causing systematic under-reporting of drug concentrations distorting TK (safety) and PK/PD (efficacy).

rules.requirement: When surrogate matrix is used, applicants must demonstrate it does not introduce additional matrix effects or alter analyte stability compared to authentic matrix; this supports 3Rs principles.
rules.rationale: Surrogate matrix validation is quality-driven but linked to scientific validity and ethical principles of nonclinical safety study conduct.

rules.requirement: When ISR fails, root-cause investigation is required; if root cause is stability or handling issue, impact on all study samples must be assessed and regulatory authority notified if pivotal safety or efficacy data is affected.
rules.rationale: ISR failure investigation is quality-triggered but notification requirement when pivotal data affected makes it a direct regulatory safety and efficacy event.

rules.requirement: Stock solution stability must be verified for both drug and internal standard; degraded stocks introduce systematic bias into all study sample results corrupting all safety and efficacy conclusions from affected runs.
rules.rationale: Stock solution integrity is lab quality control but degraded stocks cascade to systematically bias all safety and efficacy data sets.

rules.requirement: For FDC products where multiple analytes are measured in same run, stability of each analyte must be assessed in presence of co-formulated drugs; co-analyte-mediated degradation must be excluded as bias source.
rules.rationale: Combination stability (quality assessment) context is FDC clinical studies where bioequivalence (efficacy) and DDI safety are simultaneously evaluated.

rules.requirement: Under the Animal Rule, when clinical efficacy studies are not feasible, drug concentrations in animals serve as primary basis for extrapolating efficacy to humans; bioanalytical methods must be validated to M10 standards with cross-species selectivity.
rules.rationale: Animal Rule is the most extreme quality-safety-efficacy intersection: quality bioanalytical measurement in animals becomes sole basis for human efficacy claims.

rules.requirement: Reinjection reproducibility must be distinguished from processed sample stability; reinjection is only acceptable if validated, otherwise it introduces quality risk affecting clinical study data integrity.
rules.rationale: Unvalidated reinjection corrupts study sample data feeding into both safety adverse event monitoring and efficacy PK/PD characterisation.

rules.requirement: Analyte stability in presence of co-administered drugs must be assessed when samples are collected from patients receiving concomitant medications; degradation caused by in-sample DDI is a quality failure with direct PK profile impact.
rules.rationale: In-sample DDI is analytical quality issue connected to clinical polypharmacy context where safety (DDI adverse events) and efficacy (altered PK) are corrupted simultaneously.
